title: Prevention and Management of Carcinoid Crisis

items.title: Why classic carcinoid syndrome requires hepatic metastases
items.text: Midgut tumors must metastasize to liver or lung before secreted amines reach the central circulation in concentrations causing flushing, diarrhea, and carcinoid heart disease—gastric and bronchial variants bypass this via direct venous drainage.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "Classic carcinoid syndrome does not develop until a tumor has metastasized to the liver and/or lung, and the hormonal products released by the tumor reach the central venous circulation in substantial concentrations."

items.title: How carcinoid crisis presents and why it kills
items.text: Crisis is a severe episode of flushing, hypotension, tachyarrhythmia, confusion, and respiratory distress that can progress to cardiovascular collapse—early recognition and treatment are essential.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Carcinoid crisis is defined as a severe episode of flushing, hypotension, tachyarrhythmia, confusion, and respiratory distress."

items.title: Precipitants of carcinoid crisis ranked by frequency
items.text: Most to least common: carcinoid heart disease, anesthesia/surgery, interventional therapy, radionuclide therapy, examination, serotonergic drugs (e.g., clomipramine, amitriptyline), biopsy—crisis can also occur spontaneously.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Risk factors for or precipitating events for carcinoid crisis include, most common to least common, the presence of carcinoid heart disease, anesthesia or surgery, interventional therapy, radionuclide therapy, examination, medication (eg, serotonin drugs such as clomipramine and amitriptyline), and biopsy."

items.title: How to treat carcinoid crisis acutely
items.text: Somatostatin analogues are cornerstone therapy; add histamine blockers when histamine secretion is possible, vasopressin or phenylephrine for hypotension, and selective α/β blockers for hypertension—not β-blockers alone in hypotensive crisis.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "The treatment for carcinoid crisis and syndrome includes somatostatin analogues, histamine-receptor blockers, vasopressin or phenylephrine for significant hypotension, and α-adrenergic and β-adrenergic receptor blockers for hypertension."

items.title: Gastric carcinoid variant: histamine-driven flushing
items.text: Gastric carcinoids often lack aromatic L-amino acid decarboxylase and thus secrete less serotonin—diarrhea and cardiac lesions are unusual; histamine causes patchy, pruritic, serpiginous red flushes.
items.reference: Gastric Variant Syndrome: "gastric carcinoids can produce large amounts of histamine, which has been shown to have primary role in the atypical flushing and pruritus associated with these tumors."

items.title: Bronchial carcinoid syndrome features
items.text: Bronchial tumors produce less serotonin than midgut lesions; prolonged severe flushing, disorientation, lacrimation, hypotension, diarrhea, and asthma can occur—~1–2% secrete ACTH causing Cushing syndrome.
items.reference: Bronchial Carcinoid Variant Syndrome: "The symptoms of bronchial carcinoid syndrome may be atypical, with episodes of severe and prolonged flushing, lasting hours to days."

items.title: Why hindgut tumors are usually hormonally silent
items.text: Up to 90% of carcinoid syndrome arises from metastatic midgut tumors; bronchial tumors cause syndrome in ~10%; nearly all hindgut tumors are hormonally silent.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "nearly all hindgut tumors are hormonally silent."

items.title: Why carcinoid syndrome diagnosis is clinical
items.text: As many as 40 substances may be secreted; relative contributions to each syndrome component are unknown—diagnosis rests on symptoms and signs, not a single biomarker.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "Thus, the diagnosis of carcinoid syndrome and crisis is clinical based on the symptoms and signs."

items.title: Pitfall: unclear role of prophylactic somatostatin analogues
items.text: Heterogeneous definitions of crisis and limited evidence leave the role of prophylactic octreotide before procedures debated—identify at-risk patients and ensure rapid treatment capability.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "The role of prophylactic somatostatin analogues in the treatment of carcinoid crisis is unclear."

items.title: How to manage intra-procedural carcinoid crisis during liver ablation
items.text: Octreotide bolus plus continuous infusion with antihistamine and vasopressin for hypotension—β-blockers worsen hypotension; antihistamine alone is insufficient.
items.reference: Case 2: "This patient has classic signs of carcinoid crisis that is best managed with octreotide bolus and drip, vasopressin for hypotension, and antihistamine (Answer B)"

items.title: Why more clinicians will encounter NETs
items.text: Neuroendocrine tumor incidence is increasing; most carcinoids arise in the gastrointestinal tract with indolent course but potential liver and lung metastases.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "The incidence of neuroendocrine tumors (commonly referred to carcinoid) is increasing, so many health care providers will encounter patients with neuroendocrine tumors."

items.title: How to avoid carcinoid crisis in high-risk patients
items.text: The cornerstone is identifying at-risk patients (functioning tumors, carcinoid heart disease) and ensuring rapid, effective treatment is available before anesthesia, surgery, or radionuclide therapy.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "The cornerstone of avoiding carcinoid crisis is identifying at-risk patients and ensuring rapid and effective treatment."

items.question: During cryoablation of liver NET metastases, a patient develops flushing, BP 70/30, and bronchospasm. Best management?
options: Octreotide bolus and infusion, antihistamine, and vasopressin
items.explanation: Carcinoid crisis requires somatostatin analogue plus vasopressor support; β-blockers and phentolamine worsen hypotension.
items.reference: Case 2: "octreotide bolus and drip, vasopressin for hypotension, and antihistamine (Answer B)"

items.question: Classic midgut carcinoid syndrome most commonly includes:
options: Episodic flushing, diarrhea, and carcinoid heart disease
items.explanation: Hallmarks are flushing, diarrhea, valvular heart disease, and less often bronchospasm.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "The clinical hallmarks of classic carcinoid syndrome include episodic flushing, diarrhea, cardiac valvular disease, and, less frequently, bronchospasm."

items.question: Gastric carcinoid syndrome flushes are typically:
options: Patchy, serpiginous, intensely pruritic, and cherry red
Absent because all gastric carcinoids are nonsecretory
items.explanation: Histamine-mediated atypical flushes differ from classic midgut syndrome.
items.reference: Gastric Variant Syndrome: "the flushes may be patchy, sharply demarcated, serpiginous, and cherry red; they are also intensely pruritic."

items.question: Which tumor location most often causes classic carcinoid syndrome?
options: Nonfunctioning pancreatic NET without liver spread
items.explanation: Up to 90% of syndrome cases involve metastatic midgut primaries.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "In up to 90% of patients, the development of carcinoid syndrome is associated with metastatic tumors originating in the midgut."

items.question: The most common risk factor for carcinoid crisis is:
items.explanation: Carcinoid heart disease ranks first among listed precipitants.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Risk factors for or precipitating events for carcinoid crisis include, most common to least common, the presence of carcinoid heart disease"

items.question: Which medication class can precipitate carcinoid crisis?
items.explanation: Serotonin-active drugs are listed precipitants.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "medication (eg, serotonin drugs such as clomipramine and amitriptyline)"

items.question: Bronchial carcinoids causing carcinoid syndrome most often have:
items.explanation: Syndrome is rare in localized bronchial tumors but present in >80% with liver metastases.
items.reference: Bronchial Carcinoid Variant Syndrome: "it can be present in more than 80% of patients with liver metastases."

items.question: In carcinoid crisis with hypotension, which agent should be avoided?
items.explanation: β-blockers worsen hypotension in crisis.
items.reference: Case 2: "The patient has hypotension, and β-adrenergic blockers (Answer A) would make it worse."

items.question: Why gastric carcinoids rarely cause diarrhea or carcinoid heart disease:
options: They are frequently deficient in aromatic L-amino acid decarboxylase
They always metastasize to bone first
They are uniformly benign and nonsecretory
items.explanation: Failure to convert tryptophan to serotonin reduces classic syndrome features.
items.reference: Gastric Variant Syndrome: "gastric carcinoids are usually unable to convert the dietary tryptophan into serotonin, because they are frequently deficient in the enzyme aromatic L-amino acid decarboxylase."

items.question: Early symptoms of carcinoid tumors often include:
options: Nonspecific abdominal pain, diarrhea, and intermittent obstruction
Acute Cushing syndrome in all patients
items.explanation: Vague GI symptoms delay diagnosis in many patients.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "The early symptoms of carcinoid tumor are nonspecific and may include abdominal pain, diarrhea, intermittent intestinal obstruction"

items.question: Hypertension during carcinoid crisis may be treated with:
options: β-blockers as monotherapy regardless of BP
items.explanation: Main conclusions list α- and β-blockers for hypertension in crisis/syndrome.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "α-adrenergic and β-adrenergic receptor blockers for hypertension."

items.question: Extraintestinal carcinoids can cause syndrome without metastatic disease because:
options: Secreted substances gain direct access to central venous circulation
The liver inactivates all their products completely
items.explanation: Direct venous drainage bypasses first-pass hepatic metabolism.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Extraintestinal carcinoids can cause the syndrome in the absence of metastatic disease, because the substances that they secrete gain direct access to the central venous circulation."

items.question: Pellagra in carcinoid syndrome results from:
options: Excess tryptophan metabolism by the tumor
items.explanation: Tryptophan shunting to serotonin synthesis depletes niacin pathways.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "The effects of these chemicals, collectively referred to as the carcinoid syndrome, are diverse and include cutaneous flushing, diarrhea, carcinoid heart disease, bronchoconstriction, hypotension, hypertension, and pellagra."

items.statement: Carcinoid syndrome and crisis symptoms may be nonspecific, contributing to delayed diagnosis.
items.explanation: Listed as a barrier to optimal practice.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "Carcinoid syndrome and crisis symptoms may be nonspecific."

items.statement: Nearly all hindgut carcinoid tumors cause classic carcinoid syndrome.
items.explanation: Hindgut tumors are usually hormonally silent.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "nearly all hindgut tumors are hormonally silent."

items.statement: Carcinoid crisis can occur spontaneously without a procedural trigger.
items.explanation: Spontaneous crisis is acknowledged in main conclusions.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Carcinoid crisis can also occur spontaneously."

items.statement: Approximately 1–2% of bronchial carcinoids are associated with Cushing syndrome from ectopic ACTH.
items.explanation: Both typical and atypical bronchial carcinoids may secrete ACTH.
items.reference: Bronchial Carcinoid Variant Syndrome: "Approximately 1% to 2% of bronchial carcinoids (both typical and atypical) are associated with Cushing syndrome due to ectopic production of ACTH."

items.statement: Blood transfusion is appropriate first-line therapy for carcinoid crisis hypotension.
items.explanation: Case 2 explicitly rejects transfusion—patient is not bleeding.
items.reference: Case 2: "A blood transfusion (Answer D) is incorrect, as the patient is not having active bleeding."

items.statement: Peptide receptor radionuclide therapy is a listed precipitant of carcinoid crisis.
items.explanation: Radionuclide therapy is among risk factors.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "radionuclide therapy"

items.statement: Gastric carcinoids typically produce large amounts of serotonin causing classic diarrhea.
items.explanation: ALAD deficiency limits serotonin production; histamine drives atypical flushing.
items.reference: Gastric Variant Syndrome: "the diarrhea and cardiac lesions, which are common features of classic midgut carcinoid syndrome, are unusual in the gastric variant of the syndrome."

items.statement: Understanding the biochemical link between tumor and syndrome prevents misattributing isolated symptoms to non-neoplastic causes.
items.explanation: Main conclusions emphasize avoiding isolated functional misdiagnosis.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "It is very important to understand the biochemical and pathophysiological link between the carcinoid tumor and the carcinoid syndrome, because the physician may be easily misled into thinking the patient has isolated functional problems rather than a syndrome caused by a tumor."

items.assertion: Assertion: Classic carcinoid syndrome usually requires liver metastases from midgut primaries.
items.reason: Reason: Hepatic first-pass metabolism otherwise degrades secreted amines before they reach systemic circulation.
items.explanation: Both true—standard pathophysiology of midgut NET syndrome.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "Classic carcinoid syndrome does not develop until a tumor has metastasized to the liver and/or lung"

items.assertion: Assertion: Octreotide is central to carcinoid crisis management.
items.reason: Reason: β-adrenergic blockers alone are safe first-line therapy in hypotensive crisis.
items.explanation: Assertion true; β-blockers alone are contraindicated in hypotension.
items.reference: Case 2: "β-adrenergic blockers (Answer A) would make it worse."

items.assertion: Assertion: Gastric carcinoid flushes are often histamine-mediated and pruritic.
items.reason: Reason: Gastric carcinoids convert abundant dietary tryptophan to serotonin causing classic flushes.
items.explanation: Assertion true; reason false—ALAD deficiency limits serotonin.
items.reference: Gastric Variant Syndrome: "gastric carcinoids can produce large amounts of histamine, which has been shown to have primary role in the atypical flushing and pruritus"

items.assertion: Assertion: Carcinoid heart disease is the most common risk factor for crisis.
items.reason: Reason: Carcinoid heart disease is more common than anesthesia as a precipitant.
items.explanation: Both true per ordered risk-factor list.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "most common to least common, the presence of carcinoid heart disease, anesthesia or surgery"

items.assertion: Assertion: Antihistamine alone is insufficient for carcinoid crisis with hypotension.
items.reason: Reason: Antihistamine only blocks histamine effects and does not treat hypotension.
items.explanation: Both true per case 2 explanation.
items.reference: Case 2: "An antihistamine (Answer C) would only block the effect of histamine and would not treat the hypotension."

items.assertion: Assertion: Bronchial carcinoids can cause atypical prolonged flushing.
items.reason: Reason: Bronchial carcinoids produce more serotonin than midgut carcinoids.
items.explanation: Assertion true; reason false—bronchial tumors produce less serotonin.
items.reference: Bronchial Carcinoid Variant Syndrome: "Bronchial carcinoids produce less serotonin than do midgut carcinoids"

items.assertion: Assertion: Tumor biopsy can precipitate carcinoid crisis.
items.reason: Reason: Biopsy is among listed precipitating events for crisis.
items.explanation: Both true.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "biopsy. Carcinoid crisis can also occur spontaneously."

items.assertion: Assertion: Clinicians will increasingly encounter patients with neuroendocrine tumors.
items.reason: Reason: NET incidence is decreasing worldwide.
items.explanation: Assertion true; incidence is increasing, not decreasing.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "The incidence of neuroendocrine tumors (commonly referred to carcinoid) is increasing"
